courses.name: Engineering Mathematics I
courses.purpose: The basic objective of Calculus is to relate small-scale (differential) quantities to large-scale (integrated) quantities. This is accomplished by means of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. Students should demonstrate an understanding of the integral as a cumulative sum, of the derivative as a rate of change, and of the inverse relationship between integration and differentiation.
courses.description: Functions, limit and continuity, derivative, applications of derivative, integral, applications of integral, transcendental functions.
learning_outcomes: Use both the definition of derivative as a limit and the rules of differentiation to differentiate functions.
Sketch the graph of a function using asymptotes, critical points, and the derivative test for increasing/decreasing and concavity properties.
Set up max/min problems and use differentiation to solve them.
Set up related rates problems and use differentiation to solve them.
Evaluate integrals by using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
Apply integration to compute areas and volumes by slicing, volumes of revolution, arclength, and surface areas of revolution.
Evaluate integrals using techniques of integration, such as substitution, inverse substitution, partial fractions and integration by parts.
Set up and solve first order differential equations using separation of variables.
Use L'Hôpital's rule.

Hafta 2: Limit and Continuity
Hafta 3: Limit and Continuity
Hafta 4: Derivatives
Hafta 5: Derivatives
Hafta 6: Derivatives and Applications
Hafta 7: Derivatives and Applications
Hafta 8: Midterm Exam
Hafta 9: Integration
Hafta 10: Integration
Hafta 11: Integration and Applications
Hafta 12: Integration and Applications
Hafta 13: Transcendental Functions
Hafta 14: Integration techniques
Hafta 15: L’Hopital’s Rule
Hafta 16: Final Exam

R.A. Adams, Calculus: A Complete Course, 8th revised ed., Prentice Hall, 2013

courses.description: Physics and measurements, vectors, kinematics and dynamics in one and two dimensions, work-energy and conservation of energy, linear momentum and collisions, rotational motion, angular momentum, equilibrium, gravitation, oscillating motion, waves.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate conceptual understanding of the fundamental forces of nature and the laws of dynamics.
Realize importance of physics and the scientific method for advancement of technology and human life.
Analyze problems using the laws of mechanics and the conservation laws of nature.
Gain knowledge and skills for modeling and solving variety of physics and engineering problems.
Perform experiments, make measurements, analyse data and make calculations to reach meaningful results; present such activities as a scientific report.

courses.name: Programming and Computation 1
courses.purpose: This course introduces fundamental programming concepts and computational thinking. It is designed to be language-agnostic, allowing instructors to teach using their preferred programming language, such as C or Python. The course covers basic programming constructs, problem-solving techniques, and introductory algorithms and data structures.
courses.description: This course introduces students to the fundamentals of programming, starting with an overview of programming concepts and setting up the development environment. Students will explore block-based programming using Scratch, progressing from basic to advanced concepts, including event-driven programming. The course transitions into foundational problem-solving techniques through pseudocode and flowcharts. Core programming principles such as variables, data types, mathematical expressions, arrays, control flow constructs, loops, and functions are covered in-depth. Advanced topics include recursion and an introduction to object-oriented programming (OOP), emphasizing classes, inheritance, and polymorphism through practical implementation. The course integrates hands-on projects and tutorials to reinforce learning, culminating in comprehensive reviews to prepare for mid-term and final assessments.
learning_outcomes: An ability to apply knowledge of science, mathematics, and engineering.
An ability to design programs and algorithms
An ability to work with multi-disciplinary teams.
An ability to identify, formulate, and solve engineering problems.
Take responsibility to solve unpredictable and complex problems encountered in applications as an individual and as a member of a team
Plan and manage activities in teamwork
An ability to use the techniques, skills, and modern engineering tools necessary for engineering practice.
Can do research on interdisciplinary fields.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Programming; Introduction to the course and syllabus overview. What is a program? What is a programming language? Historical context and evolution of programming languages. Tutorial on setting up the programming environment and IDE installation.

Hafta 2: Scratch - Basics of Block-Based Programming; Introduction to Scratch or Blockly. Understanding basic programming concepts using block-based programming. Creating simple projects to illustrate basic concepts.
Hafta 3: Scratch - Advanced Concepts; Developing more complex projects using Scratch or Blockly. Introduction to event-driven programming. Transitioning from block-based to text-based programming.
Hafta 4: Introduction to Pseudocode and Flowchart; Introduction to Pseudocode and Its Importance. Writing Algorithms in Pseudocode. Translating Pseudocode into a Programming Language.

Hafta 5: Variables and Data Types; Understanding variables, constants, and data types. Declaring and using variables in a chosen programming language. Tutorial on variable declarations and type usage.

Hafta 6: Mathematical Expressions; Arithmetic operators and their usage. Writing and evaluating mathematical expressions. Understanding operator precedence. Tutorial on constructing mathematical expressions.
Hafta 7: Arrays; Introduction to arrays and their significance. Declaring, initializing, and accessing array elements. Tutorial on basic array operations.
Hafta 8: Mid-Term

Hafta 9: Control Flow Constructs: sequence, selection, and repetition; Conditional Statements. In-depth look at if, else if, else statements. Writing nested conditional statements. Tutorial on using conditional statements to solve problems.
Hafta 10: Loops; Introduction to loops: while, for, and do-while loops. Using loops for iteration and repetitive tasks. Tutorial on loop control statements: break and continue.
Hafta 11: Functions; Understanding function definition and invocation. Parameters and return values in functions. Tutorial on writing and using functions effectively.
Hafta 12: Recursion; Basic concepts of recursion. Writing and understanding recursive functions. Examples and applications of recursion. Tutorial on debugging recursive functions.

Hafta 13: Object-Oriented Programming – Fundamentals – 1; Class Fundamentals. Introduction to classes and objects. Understanding the concept of instances. Creating and using classes in a chosen programming language. Practical examples of class implementation. Inheritance. Understanding inheritance and its importance. Implementing inheritance in practice. Examples of single and multiple inheritance. Tutorial on creating and using derived classes.

Hafta 14: Object-Oriented Programming – Fundamentals – 2; Polymorphism. Introduction to polymorphism and its benefits. Implementing polymorphism with method overriding. Examples of polymorphism in a chosen programming language. Practical applications of polymorphism. Practical Implementation. Combining classes, inheritance, and polymorphism in a project. Step-by-step guide to designing a simple OOP-based project. Hands-on practice with real-world scenarios. Debugging and testing OOP code.
Hafta 15: Finals; Comprehensive review of all course materials. Addressing student questions and clarifying concepts. Preparation for the final exam.
Hafta 16: Final Exam
resources: C: How to Program, International Edition H. Deitel, P. Deitel, Prentice Hall
Introduction to Programming in Python: An Interdisciplinary Approach / Robert Dondero, Kevin Wayne, Robert Sedgewick

Use academic language structures and vocabulary

courses.name: Introduction to University Life
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to equip students with essential knowledge and skills for successfully adapting to university life while fostering their ability to thrive in an academic environment. It also seeks to introduce and enhance digital competencies, enabling students to utilize technology and software tools effectively in their academic pursuits. The course further provides foundational knowledge in Computer Science and Software Engineering to prepare students for both academic and professional journeys.
courses.description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to university life and foundational knowledge in computer and software engineering. It covers the OSTIM Foundation and university systems, equipping students with essential tools like the Student Information System (SIS) and introducing them to academic and administrative units. Students will also gain basic IT skills, including proficiency in office software (Word, Excel, PowerPoint), and learn foundational concepts such as number systems, algorithms, and flowcharts. In addition, the course emphasizes teamwork, the 4-Year Innovation Pathway, database management systems, computer networks, software types, and software development processes like Agile and Waterfall. Students will also develop their presentation skills through short talks on trending topics in computer and software sciences.
learning_outcomes: Understand the fundamental principles of university life, including the structure of academic and administrative units and the services provided.
Demonstrate proficiency in using essential university systems and tools, such as the Student Information System (SIS), email platforms, and digital communication resources.
Recognize the basic concepts of computer and software engineering, including key subfields and potential career pathways.
Develop teamwork skills and understand the importance of collaboration in academic and professional environments.
Apply digital tools such as Microsoft Word, Excel, and PowerPoint for academic and professional tasks.
Understand number systems (binary, octal, decimal, hexadecimal) and their relevance in computer science.
Create and analyze algorithms and flowcharts to solve problems systematically.
Understand the basic concepts of database management systems, including database structure and SQL queries.
Explain the fundamentals of computer networks, including protocols, IP addressing, and LAN/WAN concepts.
Identify different types of software and understand licensing and distribution models.
Demonstrate knowledge of software development methodologies, including Agile and Waterfall, and their applications in real-world projects.

courses.name: Linear Algebra with Engineering Applications
courses.purpose: This is an introductory course in linear algebra. The aim of this course is to teach the mathematical fundamentals of linear algebra in a way that illustrates their relevance to computer science. Students use mathematical concepts to understand facts about computers and use computers to improve their understanding of mathematics. The course also shows how linear algebra is applied in various areas of computer science.
courses.description: Vectors, matrices, linear equations, vector spaces and subspaces, orthogonality, determinants, eigenvalues and eigenvectors, linear transformations, complex vectors and matrices, numerical linear algebra.
learning_outcomes: Define basic terms and concepts of matrices, vectors and complex numbers
Apply the matrix calculus in solving a system of linear algebraic equations
Analyze the solution set of a system of linear equations.
Generalize the concepts of a real (complex) vector space to an arbitrary finite dimensional vector space.
Investigate properties of vector spaces and subspaces using by linear transformations.
Determine whether a subset of a vector space is linear dependent.
Express linear transformation between vector spaces.
Represent linear transformations by matrices.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to vectors, vectors and linear combinations, algebraic properties, lengths and dot products, matrices
Hafta 2: Solving linear equations, vectors and linear equations, elimination, row echelon form.
Hafta 3: Solving linear equations, vectors and linear equations, elimination, row echelon form
Hafta 4: Matrix operations, rules of matrix operations, independence, transposes, inverse matrices, factorization and permutations, cofactors, determinant, volumes
Hafta 5: Matrix operations, rules of matrix operations, independence, transposes, inverse matrices, factorization and permutations, cofactors, determinant, volumes
Hafta 6: Vector spaces and subspaces, null space, dimensions, vector independence
Hafta 7: Orthogonality, least squares approximations, orthonormal bases and Gram-Schmidt
Hafta 8: Midterm Exam
Hafta 9: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors, diagonalization, systems of differential equations
Hafta 10: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors, diagonalization, systems of differential equations
Hafta 11: Symmetric matrices, diagonalization of symmetric matrices, quadratic forms, positive definite matrices
Hafta 12: Symmetric matrices, diagonalization of symmetric matrices, quadratic forms, positive definite matrices
Hafta 13: Linear transformations (mapping), the matrix of a linear transformation
Hafta 14: Complex vectors and matrices, complex numbers, systems with complex numbers, vector spaces
Hafta 15: Eigenvectors and inner products in complex vector spaces, hermitian matrices and unitary diagonalization
Hafta 16: Final Exam
resources: G. Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, 5th Ed., Wellesly-Cambridge Press, 2016.
S. Lipschutz and M. Lipson, Schaum's Outline of Linear Algebra, 6th Edition. New York: McGraw-Hill Education, 2017.

courses.name: Principles of Atatürk and History of Turkish Revolution I
courses.purpose: This course aims to help students understand the Turkish Revolution, Atatürk’s thought, and the principles of Atatürk by comparing them with earlier Turkish and world history. It also seeks to help students connect historical developments with their own field of expertise and view contemporary world and Turkish issues from a broader perspective.
courses.description: The course covers the concept of revolution, the factors leading to the Turkish Revolution, the collapse of the Ottoman Empire, efforts to save the empire, ideological movements, World War I, Mustafa Kemal’s arrival in Anatolia and the congresses, the opening of the Grand National Assembly of Turkey, the War of Independence, foreign policy, the Mudanya Armistice, and the Lausanne Conference.
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to establish a connection between their field of expertise and history.
Students will gain general knowledge about the recent history of Turkey.
Students will gain knowledge about the founding philosophy and basic principles of the Republic of Turkey.
Students will acquire the necessary background about the development of democracy in Turkey.
Students will gain general cultural knowledge about the 20th century world, major international developments, and the decisive developments that affect the present day.
Students will gain knowledge about the international problems of contemporary Turkey and their sources.
Students will develop the ability to compare past and present conditions and build a bridge of knowledge.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to the ATA 101 course and the course overview; introduction and examination of concepts and processes such as revolution, uprising, and rebellion that differentiate societies by affecting them politically, socio-economically, and culturally; the equivalents of these concepts in Turkish history before the 20th century.

Hafta 2: General evaluation of major political, social, economic, and cultural developments in the 18th and 19th centuries along the line of Western modernization: Europe after the Age of Discoveries, the rise of the bourgeoisie and capitalism, the French Revolution, the Congress of Vienna, and the establishment of the balance of political alliances in Europe.

Hafta 3: Examination of the Ottoman state system and social model in the 18th and 19th centuries, and analysis of the political, economic, and social situation of the Ottoman Empire within the existing world order.
Hafta 4: Process and factors leading to the Turkish Revolution I: balance policies and ideological movements within the efforts to save the Ottoman Empire.

Hafta 5: Process and factors leading to the Turkish Revolution II: reform movements within the modernization problem of the traditional Ottoman structure, the struggle of the Young Turks, and the First and Second Constitutional Monarchy periods.

Hafta 6: The struggle for aviation from Ibn Firnas to Hazerfen; the Tripoli War and the first airplanes; the conditions that led to imperialist competition at the beginning of the 20th century, the Tripoli War, the Balkan Wars, and World War I.
Hafta 7: Midterm exam.

Hafta 8: The end of the age of empires: World War I, the Ottoman Empire during the war years, and the Armenian Question; the establishment of a new world order after World War I and the shift toward nation-states.
Hafta 9: After the Armistice of Mudros: the Ottoman State and Mustafa Kemal Pasha, resistance movements against occupations in Anatolia and the Kuva-yi Milliye, local congress governments.
Hafta 10: Mustafa Kemal Pasha’s arrival in Samsun and organization efforts based on congresses I: Amasya Circular, Erzurum Congress, and Sivas Congress.

Hafta 11: Mustafa Kemal Pasha’s arrival in Samsun and organization efforts based on congresses II: Amasya Meetings, the arrival of the Representative Committee in Ankara, the convening of the last Ottoman Chamber of Deputies, the National Pact decisions, and the official occupation of Istanbul.
Hafta 12: From the toy (kurultay) to the Grand National Assembly of Turkey; the parliamentary tradition among Turks.
Hafta 13: The 1921 Constitution, the power struggle between Istanbul and Ankara, and the Treaty of Sèvres.

Hafta 14: The War of Independence: the Eastern Front, the Southern Front, the Western Front, the Battle of Gediz, the establishment of the regular army, the First İnönü, the Second İnönü, the Kütahya/Eskişehir Battles, the Battle of Sakarya, the Great Offensive, and the Commander-in-Chief Battle.
Hafta 15: The Armistice of Mudanya.
Hafta 16: Final exam.
resources: Ali İhsan Gencer, Sabahattin Özel, Türk İnkılap Tarihi, İstanbul: Der Yayınları, 2011.
R. Turan, M. Safran, N. Hayta, Atatürk İlkeleri ve İnkılap Tarihi, Ankara: Okutman Yayıncılık, 2012.
Fatma Acun, Atatürk ve Türk İnkılâp Tarihi, Ankara: Siyasal Kitabevi, 2011.
M. Gönlübol, Ö. Kürkçüoğlu, Olaylarla Türk Dış Politikası (1919-1995), Ankara: Siyasal Kitabevi, 1996.

courses.name: Fundementals of Entrepreneurship
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to transfer basic information about entrepreneurship to students and establish project groups to realize the project ideas they develop within the framework of these principles. It also aims to help students understand the place and importance of entrepreneurship in the global economic system and to apply business model development practices to clarify and test project ideas.
courses.description: This course covers the basic concepts, nature, scope, and importance of entrepreneurship. It includes opportunity recognition, idea generation, feasibility analysis, business model development, industry and competitor analysis, business plan preparation, family business, marketing and financing issues, and ethical and legal foundations. Students also prepare in-class presentations and work on a project idea.
learning_outcomes: To master the basic concepts of entrepreneurship in the literature.
To understand the place and importance of entrepreneurship in the global economic system.
To learn to develop business and project ideas effectively thanks to the principles of entrepreneurship.
To discover the value proposition and other important dimensions of the project and business idea through the principles of developing a Business Model.
To analyze the value of this idea within the scope of entrepreneurship, by developing the project idea in a technology-based field.
To learn to work and be organized as a team.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction/Syllabus Review; Entrepreneurship Center; Aim of the Course; Teaching Techniques of the Course; The Concept, Aim and Facilities, The Application Process; OSTİMTECH Entrepreneurship Center

Hafta 2: Nature and Scope of Entrepreneurship; What Is Entrepreneurship?; Why become an entrepreneur?; Types of Entrepreneurs; Types of Start-Up Firms; The Entrepreneurial Process; Impact of Entrepreneurial Firms

Hafta 3: Nature and Scope of Entrepreneurship; What Is Entrepreneurship?; Why become an entrepreneur?; Types of Entrepreneurs; Types of Start-Up Firms; The Entrepreneurial Process; Impact of Entrepreneurial Firms
Hafta 4: Recognizing Opportunities and Generating Ideas; Identifying and Recognizing Opportunities; Finding Gaps in the Marketplace; Techniques for Generating Ideas
Hafta 5: Feasibility Analysis; Product/Service Feasibility Analysis; Industry/Target Market Feasibility Analysis; Organizational Feasibility Analysis; Financial Feasibility Analysis
Hafta 6: Developing an Effective Business Model; The Importance and Diversity of Business Models; Components of an Effective Business Model; Strategic Resources
Hafta 7: Industry and Competitor Analysis; The Five Forces Model; Industry Types and the Opportunities They Offer; Sources of Competitive Intelligence
Hafta 8: MIDTERM EXAM

courses.name: Programming and Computation II
courses.purpose: This course aims to build a strong foundation in C and C++ programming, including procedural and object-oriented paradigms. It prepares students to develop secure, modular, and scalable software using core language features, data structures, file handling, and the C++ Standard Library.
courses.description: This course provides a structured introduction to C and C++ programming, covering both fundamental and advanced topics. Students begin with character and string manipulation, formatted input/output, data structures, dynamic memory allocation, and file processing in C. The course also explores bitwise operations, preprocessor directives, and secure programming techniques for writing efficient and maintainable code. In the second part, the course transitions to C++ programming, introducing functions, references, templates, and object-oriented programming concepts such as encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism. The C++ Standard Library is used to develop scalable and modular applications. Practical exercises, coding projects, and assessments reinforce the essential concepts and skills needed for real-world software development.
learning_outcomes: Understand the fundamental principles of C++ programming, including syntax, semantics, and object-oriented concepts such as encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism.
Analyze and apply advanced features such as operator overloading, exception handling, and dynamic memory management for robust and efficient applications.
Design modular and reusable programs using classes, objects, templates, and inheritance hierarchies to address real-world problems.
Use C++ Standard Library components such as vectors, streams, and file processing to manage data effectively and perform input/output operations.
Develop object-oriented systems by applying design principles and leveraging the Unified Modeling Language (UML) for system visualization and architecture.

courses.name: Object Oriented Programming
courses.purpose: The course aims to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of the fundamental concepts of object-oriented programming, including encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction. Students will learn to design and implement classes and objects that effectively model real-world entities and relationships. The course emphasizes the importance of code reusability through inheritance and composition, promoting efficient software development practices. Students will apply object-oriented techniques to solve complex programming problems, focusing on algorithm design and data structures. Debugging and testing skills will be developed to ensure the reliability and correctness of object-oriented programs. Students will also analyze and implement OOP concepts in real-world applications using C# programming language.

courses.name: Data Structures and Algorithms
courses.purpose: The primary goal of this course is to introduce students to the fundamental concepts of data structures and algorithms, with a focus on their design, analysis, and theoretical application. The course aims to help students evaluate and choose appropriate data structures for specific problems and to reinforce these concepts with illustrative C programming examples.
courses.description: The course provides a comprehensive introduction to data structures and algorithms, focusing on their design, analysis, and applications. Topics include linear structures such as arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues, as well as non-linear structures like binary trees, AVL trees, Huffman trees, and heaps. Searching methods such as linear, binary, and interpolation search, along with sorting techniques including bubble sort, insertion sort, selection sort, quicksort, mergesort, and heapsort, are covered. Advanced topics include hashing for efficient data retrieval, graph representation and traversal (DFS, BFS, Dijkstra), and minimum spanning tree algorithms (Kruskal, Prim).
learning_outcomes: Understand the fundamental concepts of data structures and algorithms, including their importance and applications.
Analyze and evaluate the time and space complexity of algorithms using Big-O notation.
Describe and differentiate between linear and non-linear data structures, including their operations and applications.
Apply searching and sorting algorithms to solve computational problems efficiently.
Understand and implement the concepts of hashing, graph representation, and traversal techniques in problem-solving.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Data Structures and Algorithms
Hafta 2: Time Complexity - Arrays - Structures - Linked List
Hafta 3: Stacks, Queues
Hafta 4: Basic Tree Terminology - Binary Trees – Expression Trees- Traversing a Binary Tree
Hafta 5: Huffman’s Tree - Binary Search Trees, Threaded Binary Trees
Hafta 6: AVL trees
Hafta 7: Heaps
Hafta 8: Midterm Exam
Hafta 9: Searching Algorithms (Linear Search, Binary Search, Interpolation Search, Jump Search)
Hafta 10: Sorting Algorithms (Buble Sort, Insertion Sort, Selection Sort, Merge Sort, Quick Sort)
Hafta 11: Hashing Algorithm
Hafta 12: Graphs Terminology – Graphs Representation
Hafta 13: Topological Sort
Hafta 14: DFS, BFS and Dijkstra's algorithms
Hafta 15: Spanning trees-Minimum Spanning trees - Kruskal, Prim
Hafta 16: Final Exam

courses.name: Formal Languages and Automata
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide an understanding of the theory of automata, the computability of problems, and the complexity of computations. It also introduces different models of computation and compares them.
courses.description: Deterministic finite automata, non-deterministic finite automata, regular expressions, context-free grammars, pushdown automata, Turing machines, decidability and undecidability, and P and NP classes.
learning_outcomes: Understand and construct Deterministic Finite-State Automata (DFA), Nondeterministic Finite-State Automata (NFA)
Recognize the equivalence between NFA and DFA
Understand and construct Context-Free Grammars (CFG) with simple examples
Construct push down automata accepting a given context free language
Recognize the equivalence between CFG and pushdown automata
Apply the pumping lemma to demonstrate whether or not the language is regular or context free
Understand computation through Turing Machines performing simple tasks
Solve problems using formal languages
Develop a fundamental understanding of computational theory and models including decidability and intractability
Develop fundamental understanding of complexity theory

Hafta 3: Finite Automata (FA): Deterministic FA, Nondeterministic FA

Hafta 5: Properties of Regular Languages; The pumping lemma

Hafta 10: Equivalence of PDA and CFG

Hafta 12: Turing Machines
Hafta 13: Decidability
Hafta 14: P & NP, NP-completeness
Hafta 15: P & NP, NP-completeness
Hafta 16: Final Exam

Michael Sipser, Introduction to the theory of computation.
Peter Linz, An Introduction to Formal Languages and Automata.

courses.name: Introduction to Database Systems
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to provide students with basic knowledge and skills in information systems and database management systems (DBMS). The course aims to provide students with the opportunity to gain in-depth knowledge in data management, database design, use of SQL language and data analysis. Participants will learn how information systems work in the business world and how to use them effectively, while reinforcing the fundamentals of database management and design.
courses.description: The course covers a wide range of topics, starting from the basic concepts of information systems to the history and structure of database management systems and basic commands in SQL language. Weekly lectures will focus on advanced data management techniques such as data modeling, requirements analysis, conceptual modeling, normalization, and data manipulation commands. Modern data management tools such as different types of databases, NoSQL databases, and cloud-based solutions will also be covered. In addition to theoretical knowledge, participants will be able to reinforce what they have learned through practical applications and projects and develop solutions for data management problems they may encounter in the business world.
learning_outcomes: Describe the basic components and functions of information systems: Students will be able to describe the basic components of information systems (data, software, hardware, people) and their functions.

Compare Different Information System Architectures: Students will be able to compare centralized, distributed and cloud-based information system architectures and discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each.
Write Basic Database Queries with SQL Language: Students will be able to write basic queries, insert, update and delete data in databases using SQL language.
Design a Database Using Data Modeling Techniques (ERD): Students will be able to create a database design using ERD and visualize the connections between entities and relationships correctly.

Develop an appropriate model by analyzing database requirements and normalization: Students will be able to develop an appropriate data model by analyzing database requirements and improve the design by using normalization techniques.

Hafta 12: Concurrency Control Theory: Introduction to Transactions, Concurrent access issues, ACID properties,
Hafta 13: More Transactions: Serial schedules, conflict equivalence

courses.name: Operating Systems Design and Construction
courses.purpose: This course helps students comprehend the fundamentals of operating systems, which serve as the foundational computing framework for a wide range of computer applications. It bridges the gap between user applications that require OS services and computer architecture, and supports students in designing and managing computing infrastructures as well as creating high-performance, concurrent, and systems software.
courses.description: Introduction, operating-system structures, processes, threads and concurrency, CPU scheduling, synchronization tools and examples, and deadlocks.
learning_outcomes: Recognize operating system design and implementation
Recognize how complicated software systems organize their memory and data structures
Recognize how a sophisticated software system shares resources
Recognize how a complicated software system exchanges data concurrently
Check the integrity of the data in concurrent systems
Create and put into practice algorithms for issues involving synchronization and concurrency

courses.name: Data Mining

Students will learn how to apply various data mining techniques, including classification, clustering, and association rule mining, to solve real-world problems. The course also covers data warehousing, data preprocessing, feature selection methods, and ethical considerations in data mining.

Create a clean, consistent repository of data within a data warehouse

Discover patterns and knowledge that is embedded in the data using several different data mining techniques, such as neural nets, decision trees and associative rule mining

courses.name: Workplace Experience I
courses.purpose: The objective of this course is to enable students to transition from supervised task execution to more independent project contributions within a professional engineering environment. The course aims to have students apply advanced engineering principles and analytical skills to solve real-world problems. Key objectives include developing core competencies in project management, system analysis, and process optimization; demonstrating a high level of professional autonomy and ethical responsibility; and effectively communicating technical work to diverse stakeholders. This experience serves as a critical preparation for the final year capstone project and future engineering practice.
courses.description: This course requires students to spend one full day per week at a partner company, taking on a more significant role within a project team. The content involves deeper involvement in defined project modules or medium-scale engineering tasks. Students will be responsible for analyzing existing systems, proposing and developing solutions under the guidance of a mentor, and contributing to formal project documentation. The course emphasizes the application of analytical tools, validation of results, and the preparation of a comprehensive final report and presentation that details their technical contributions, problem-solving process, and professional growth.

courses.name: Workplace Experience II
courses.purpose: The primary objective of this capstone course is for students to integrate and synthesize the comprehensive engineering knowledge acquired throughout their undergraduate studies in a real-world professional setting. The course aims to transition students from an academic to a professional mindset by having them take significant ownership of engineering tasks. Key objectives include contributing meaningfully to complex, multi-stage engineering projects, mastering advanced industry-specific tools and methodologies, demonstrating autonomy and leadership potential, and producing professional-grade documentation and presentations.
courses.description: This course requires students to spend two full days per week at a partner company, fully integrated into an engineering team. Students contribute to an ongoing engineering project and engage in problem definition, analysis, design, implementation, and validation. They are responsible for managing project components, collaborating with team members and other departments, adhering to company standards and timelines, preparing technical documentation, and delivering mid-term and final presentations on their project contributions, challenges, and outcomes.
learning_outcomes: Integrates into the company's organizational and professional culture and proposes improvements to project workflows based on observation and analysis.
Synthesizes advanced engineering principles and theories to design, develop, and manage complex engineering project components from conception to completion.
Masters and independently applies modern engineering tools, software, and analytical techniques, justifying their selection for specific project requirements.
Leads and collaborates effectively within diverse engineering teams, demonstrating initiative, managing responsibilities, and upholding professional ethics.
Produces professional-grade technical documentation and persuasively presents complex project findings to both technical and non-technical audiences.
Critically evaluates and ensures adherence to occupational health, safety regulations, and quality assurance standards throughout the project lifecycle.

courses.name: Workplace Experience III
courses.purpose: This capstone course is designed to support the student's transition from academic study to a professional engineering role. It aims to help students manage a substantial real-world project, develop autonomy and responsibility, and strengthen leadership, communication, and teamwork skills in a workplace setting.
courses.description: Students work full-time or near full-time at a partner company for the semester on a major engineering project developed with the workplace mentor. The course covers the full project lifecycle, including requirements analysis, planning, research, design, development, testing, validation, documentation, reporting, and final presentation.
learning_outcomes: Integrates advanced engineering principles and synthesizes complex information to manage and execute a significant, industry-relevant project from conception to completion.
Designs and develops innovative and robust solutions to open-ended engineering problems, demonstrating creativity, critical analysis, and adherence to technical and business constraints.
Masters the use of advanced engineering tools, software, simulation packages, and experimental methods to analyze, design, and validate complex systems.

Leads and collaborates effectively within a professional engineering team, managing responsibilities, navigating corporate dynamics, and communicating technical concepts clearly to diverse stakeholders.
Produces comprehensive, professional-grade technical documentation (reports, drawings, manuals) and delivers persuasive oral presentations that defend project outcomes.
Evaluates engineering solutions in the context of professional ethics, public health and safety, sustainability, and commercial viability, demonstrating a holistic professional judgment.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Company/Project Immersion, Finalizing Project Scope & Objectives, Detailed Project Plan Development
Hafta 2: Requirements Gathering, Stakeholder Meetings, and Initial Feasibility Analysis
Hafta 3: In-depth Literature and Technology Review, Conceptual Design and Brainstorming
Hafta 4: System-Level Design, Component Selection, and Preliminary Analysis/Simulation
Hafta 5: Detailed Design of Key Components and Sub-systems
Hafta 6: Mid-Project Progress Review with Mentor and Advisor, Design Refinement
Hafta 7: Prototyping, Manufacturing, or Implementation Phase I
Hafta 8: Prototyping, Manufacturing, or Implementation Phase II
Hafta 9: Development of Test Plans and Experimental Setup
Hafta 10: Development of Test Plans and Experimental Setup
Hafta 11: Development of Test Plans and Experimental Setup
Hafta 12: Execution of Initial Tests, Data Collection, and Performance Analysis
Hafta 13: Troubleshooting, System Optimization, and Re-testing based on initial findings
Hafta 14: Validation of Project against Initial Requirements and Objectives
Hafta 15: Documentation of Final Design, Results, and Project Outcomes
Hafta 16: Preparation of Comprehensive Final Project Report
resources: Workplace Education Guideline
Company-Specific Project Documentation and Briefs
Relevant Industry Standards, Codes, and Regulations (e.g., ISO, ASME)
Technical Manuals for Software and Equipment
Project Management and Collaboration Tools (e.g., Jira, MS Project)
prerequisites: Must be a 4th-year student and have successfully completed WEX 328
Corequisites (Workplace Experience II) and all other preceding workplace education courses

courses.name: Principles of Atatürk and History of Turkish Revolution II
Probability and Statistics for Engineers
courses.purpose: To teach engineering students the necessary probability and statistical techniques, to be able to interpret the results of statistical analysis and to make correct statistical decisions.
courses.description: Application of basic concepts in probability and statistics with engineering. Topics include descriptive and inferential statistics, probability, discrete and continuous random variables, confidence interval estimation, regression and correlation, and analysis of variance.
learning_outcomes: Define the basic concepts of probability and statistics.
Calculate types of probability, independent events, Bayes theorem and conditional probability.
Summarize and interpret engineering problems using descriptive statistics.
Solve engineering problems with inferential statistics (hypothesis testing).
Solve and interpret statistical problems using computers, in addition to the ability to calculate with formulas to solve engineering problems.

Hafta 2: Distributions and Classification: Frequency and Cumulative Frequency Distributions
Hafta 3: Measures of Central Tendency
Hafta 4: Measures of Central Dispersion

Hafta 13: Regression and Correlation Analysis
Hafta 14: One-Way Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)
Hafta 15: Two-Way Analysis of Variance
Hafta 16: General Exam

Lawrence L. Lapin (1990), Probability and Statistics for Modern Engineering, PWS-Kent Pub. Co. edition, in English - 2nd ed.

courses.name: Software Project Management
courses.purpose: This course gives an overview of project management and introduces important concepts, critical-thinking, project management skills, and tools used by engineers in managing projects. It aims to build students' awareness of engineering thinking and the project management mindset, and to help them understand how projects are managed with limited resources, how project risks are handled, and what contributes to successful project management.
courses.description: The course covers the fundamentals of project management, including project definitions, project characteristics, project history, project management professions, project manager characteristics, leadership, project life cycle, risk, traditional and agile approaches, Scrum, and organizational structures. It also addresses integration and scope management, time management, cost management and financial feasibility, resource management, team and conflict management, quality management, and agile software project management with Jira.
learning_outcomes: Gains managerial, project management oriented sensitivity, perception and awareness.
To be able to analyze by questioning, thinking critically, and to reflect this in her/his projects.
Ability to think critically, make systematic criticism, and to make analysis in this direction and to be able to reflect these in her/his professional life.
Ability to propose appropriate solutions to problems in line with the inferences and learnings.
Ability to make use of different problem solving, decision making tools and techniques.
Ability to work in groups and ability to presents project, related ideas, model, findings, outcomes.

Hafta 2: Introduction to "Software" Project Management
Hafta 3: Integration & Scope Management
Hafta 4: Time Management

courses.name: New Trends in Software Engineering
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide a comprehensive understanding of the evolution and latest trends in software engineering disciplines while introducing students to the tools, frameworks, and technologies shaping modern software development. Through assignments and projects, students will develop practical skills and foster analytical thinking by comparing traditional and modern approaches within various domains. The course enhances problem-solving abilities using cutting-edge technologies such as AI, Blockchain, and Cloud Computing and equips students with the knowledge to identify and implement best practices in software development, testing, and deployment. By exploring future trends and innovations, students will be prepared for emerging roles in the software industry.
courses.description: This syllabus is designed to cover each branch of software engineering and the latest trends. The course content spans various domains including frontend and backend development, DevOps, software testing, embedded systems, data engineering, artificial intelligence, cybersecurity, cloud computing, mobile app development, blockchain, and game development. It highlights practical applications and emerging trends like microservices, serverless computing, AI-powered tools, and decentralized systems. Students will engage with tools, frameworks, and methodologies relevant to each discipline while exploring advanced topics such as quantum computing, low-code platforms, and the future of software engineering. The syllabus integrates assignments and resources to foster a hands-on and analytical learning experience.
learning_outcomes: Students will understand the evolution of software engineering and the impact of emerging technologies on software development processes.
Students will learn how to integrate modern technologies such as artificial intelligence, machine learning, and cloud computing into software projects.
Students will comprehend the contributions of the Internet of Things (IoT), embedded systems, and autonomous technologies to software engineering.
Students will explore the advantages and challenges of approaches such as microservices architecture, DevOps, and CI/CD in software development.
Students will gain in-depth knowledge of agile software development methodologies, software test automation, and sustainable software development.
weekly_topics: Hafta 1: Introduction to Emerging Trends in Software and Computer Engineering
Hafta 2: Advancements in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning
Hafta 3: Cloud Computing: Modern Software Development and Deployment
Hafta 4: The Internet of Things (IoT), Embedded Systems, and Autonomous Technologies
Hafta 5: Quantum Computing: The Future of Computation
Hafta 6: Microservices Architecture: Scalable and Resilient Systems
Hafta 7: DevOps Practices and Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD)
Hafta 8: Blockchain Technology and Smart Contracts: Decentralized Innovation

Hafta 10: Data Science and Big Data: Extracting Value from Information
Hafta 11: Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) and UX Design: AR/VR Applications
Hafta 12: Cybersecurity and DevSecOps: Securing the Software Ecosystem
Hafta 13: Agile Software Development, Project Management, and Advances in Test Automation
Hafta 14: Green Computing and Sustainable Software Engineering
Hafta 15: Final exam

courses.name: Graduation Project II
courses.purpose: This course continues the work started in Graduation Project I and aims to guide students through the execution, completion, and presentation of a software engineering project. It also develops students' ability to solve problems, apply engineering practices, work effectively in teams, and present technical results clearly.
courses.description: The course covers the implementation of the proposed software engineering project, including the analysis, design, prototyping, simulation, or experiments specified in the project plan. It concludes with a comprehensive final report and an oral presentation.

courses.name: Algorithms Analysis
courses.purpose: Bu ders, öğrencilerin algoritmik stratejileri ve bunların verimliliklerini derinlemesine anlamalarını sağlamak için tasarlanmıştır. Öğrenciler, algoritmaların zaman ve bellek karmaşıklığını analiz etmeyi ve farklı problem türleri için uygun algoritmik yaklaşımları seçmeyi öğrenirler.
courses.description: Bu ders; sıralama ve arama algoritmaları, grafik algoritmaları, dinamik programlama, açgözlü yöntemler, amortize analiz, hesaplama karmaşıklığı teorisi, NP-tamlık ve yaklaşım algoritmaları gibi konuları kapsar. Teorik anlatımın yanı sıra kodlama ödevleri ve problem çözme oturumları ile pratik uygulamaya da yer verilir.
learning_outcomes: Algoritmaların zaman ve bellek karmaşıklığını Big-O, Big-Ω ve Big-Θ gösterimleriyle analiz eder.
Sıralama, arama ve grafik algoritmalarını uygun durumlarda uygular.
Dinamik programlama ve açgözlü yöntemler gibi temel algoritmik tasarım tekniklerini kullanır.
Amortize analiz ve hesaplama karmaşıklığı kavramlarını açıklar.
NP-tamlık ve yaklaşım algoritmaları hakkında temel düzeyde bilgi sahibi olur.

courses.name: Computer Games and Simulation
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the principles and applications of computer game development and simulation modeling. It aims to build practical understanding of how interactive games and simulations are designed, developed, and evaluated using industry-standard tools and techniques.
courses.description: Topics include game physics, animation, artificial intelligence for non-player characters, and real-time rendering. Students explore simulation techniques such as discrete-event simulation and agent-based modeling. Using tools such as Unity or Unreal Engine, they design and develop interactive games and simulations, gaining experience in game mechanics, storytelling, and user interaction. The course concludes with a team-based project in which students develop a playable game or simulation prototype.

courses.name: Introduction to Matlab Applications
courses.purpose: This course is designed to introduce students with little or no prior experience in MATLAB to the use of MATLAB for solving engineering and scientific problems.
courses.description: The course focuses on MATLAB usage for solving engineering and scientific problems. Topics include matrix operations, data visualization, control structures, and function creation. Students use MATLAB in real-world applications such as signal processing, image analysis, and control systems. The course emphasizes applied learning through laboratory work and mini-projects.
learning_outcomes: Use MATLAB to solve engineering and scientific problems.
Perform matrix operations and create functions in MATLAB.
Visualize data effectively using MATLAB tools.
Apply MATLAB in signal processing, image analysis, and control systems.
Develop algorithms and perform numerical computations.

courses.name: Mobile Programming
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with the knowledge and practical skills needed to design and develop mobile applications for modern platforms such as Android and iOS.
courses.description: This course covers mobile application design and development for platforms such as Android and iOS. Students learn mobile user interface (UI) and user experience (UX) principles, responsive design, device sensors, and data persistence. The course also addresses native development, such as Kotlin/Java for Android and Swift for iOS, as well as cross-platform frameworks like Flutter or React Native. Security, performance optimization, and application deployment are also discussed. Students gain hands-on experience by completing a series of projects that result in a fully functional mobile application.

courses.name: Artificial Neural Networks
courses.purpose: This course introduces the theory and practical applications of artificial neural networks. It aims to provide students with a foundation in neural network concepts and their use in modern machine learning and deep learning tasks.
courses.description: The course covers the theory and applications of artificial neural networks (ANN). Students learn about perceptrons, multilayer feedforward networks, backpropagation algorithms, and activation functions. Advanced topics include convolutional neural networks (CNN), recurrent neural networks (RNN), and deep learning frameworks such as TensorFlow and PyTorch. Applications such as image recognition, natural language processing, and predictive modeling are examined through hands-on projects and case studies.

courses.name: Smart Machines
courses.purpose: This course examines the design and development of intelligent systems that can perceive, reason, and act, with a focus on the integration of artificial intelligence and robotics.
courses.description: The course focuses on AI and robotics integration, covering the design and development of intelligent systems with sensing, reasoning, and action capabilities. Topics include sensor integration, control systems, path planning, and machine learning for robotics. Students work with platforms such as Arduino, Raspberry Pi, or ROS (Robot Operating System) to build and program autonomous machines. The course emphasizes real-world applications such as smart manufacturing, autonomous vehicles, and assistive robotics.

courses.name: Python Programming
courses.purpose: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to Python, one of the most versatile and widely used programming languages. It aims to help students build a strong foundation in programming and develop practical coding skills through hands-on work.
courses.description: Students learn fundamental programming concepts such as data types, control structures, functions, and object-oriented programming. The course also covers Python libraries used for data analysis (Pandas, NumPy), web development (Flask, Django), and automation. Through applied assignments and projects, students gain experience writing clean, efficient, and reusable code.

courses.name: Software Economics
courses.purpose: This course examines the economic principles underlying the software industry and helps students understand how software products and services are valued, financed, and delivered in the market.
courses.description: The course covers software pricing models, cost estimation, return on investment (ROI), open source economics, and the impact of intellectual property laws. Students analyze case studies of software companies and explore the dynamics of software markets such as SaaS, freemium models, and platform economies. The course also addresses the role of software in digital transformation and global economic development.

courses.name: Adli Bilişim Mühendisliğine Giriş
courses.purpose: Bu ders, öğrencilere dijital adli bilişim alanını ve bunun siber güvenlik ile kolluk kuvvetlerindeki rolünü tanıtmak için tasarlanmıştır. Öğrencilerin dijital delilleri toplama, koruma ve analiz etme süreçlerini anlamaları amaçlanır.
courses.description: Konular arasında adli veri elde etme, delil koruma, dosya sistemi analizi ve ağ adli bilişimi yer alır. Öğrenciler, siber suçları araştırmak, silinmiş verileri kurtarmak ve dijital izleri takip etmek için adli bilişim araçlarını kullanmayı öğrenirler. Zincirleme delil takibi ve gizlilik yasaları gibi hukuki ve etik konular da ele alınır. Uygulamalı laboratuvar çalışmaları, gerçek dünya adli bilişim soruşturmalarını simüle eder.
learning_outcomes: Dijital adli bilişimin temel kavramlarını ve siber güvenlik ile kolluk kuvvetlerindeki rolünü açıklar.
Adli veri elde etme ve delil koruma süreçlerini uygular.
Dosya sistemi ve ağ adli bilişimi tekniklerini kullanır.
Silinmiş verileri kurtarmak ve dijital izleri takip etmek için adli bilişim araçlarını kullanır.
Zincirleme delil takibi ile gizlilik yasaları gibi hukuki ve etik konuları değerlendirir.

courses.name: Web Programming
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with a comprehensive full-stack web development foundation. It prepares students to design, build, and deploy modern web applications using both front-end and back-end technologies.
courses.description: The course covers front-end technologies such as HTML5, CSS3, and JavaScript, followed by back-end development with frameworks such as Node.js, Django, or Express. Topics include RESTful APIs, database integration, authentication, and web security. Students develop dynamic and responsive web applications and deploy them through cloud platforms. The course also emphasizes modern development practices such as version control, testing, and agile methodologies.

courses.name: System Programming
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to system-level programming and the interface between software and hardware. It prepares students to write programs that interact with the operating system and to develop practical low-level programming skills.
courses.description: The course focuses on system programming topics such as system calls, memory management, process and thread control, file input/output, shell scripting, interprocess communication, and concurrency. It uses C/C++ and Unix/Linux environments, with applied laboratory work to reinforce low-level programming skills.

courses.name: E-Signature Technologies
courses.purpose: To equip students with both theoretical and practical knowledge on e-signature technologies, including coding and integration processes. By the end of the course, students will be well-prepared to meet industry demands and become highly skilled professionals.
courses.description: The role of e-signatures in projects such as e-invoice, e-prescription, e-correspondence, e-ledger, registered electronic mail, and e-archive. The structure and functioning of e-signature technology in light of ETSI standards and RFCs. Software development processes with real-world application examples.
learning_outcomes: Acquisition of problem-solving and analysis skills
Acquisition of the ability to apply basic technical skills
Programming ability acquisition
Comprehend algorithm logic
Designing and writing the program that suits the needs

courses.name: Natural Language Processing
courses.purpose: This course introduces computational techniques used to process and analyze human language. It aims to build students' understanding of how NLP methods are applied to real-world language data and applications.
courses.description: The course covers text preprocessing, tokenization, part-of-speech tagging, syntactic parsing, sentiment analysis, and language modeling. Students work with natural language processing libraries such as NLTK, spaCy, and Hugging Face Transformers. Application areas include chatbots, machine translation, and information extraction. The course combines theoretical foundations with hands-on projects using real-world datasets.

courses.name: Introduction to Data Visualization
courses.purpose: Students learn how to transform complex data into clear and compelling visual narratives.
courses.description: The course covers visual perception principles, best practices in design, and data storytelling. Students create static and interactive visualizations using tools and libraries such as Tableau, Matplotlib, Seaborn, and D3.js. They work on projects involving data cleaning, selecting appropriate visualization techniques, and presenting findings effectively.

courses.name: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the fundamental concepts and techniques of artificial intelligence.
courses.description: Topics include search algorithms, knowledge representation, logic and reasoning, machine learning, and intelligent agents. Students explore applications such as robotics, game playing, and decision support systems. The course also includes practical assignments carried out using artificial intelligence libraries and frameworks.

courses.name: Systems Analysis and Design
courses.purpose: This course aims to equip students with the methods and tools needed to analyze information systems in a structured way and to design appropriate solutions based on gathered requirements.
courses.description: This course focuses on the structured analysis and design of information systems. Students learn requirements gathering and documentation, system modeling with UML diagrams, and designing system architectures. Topics include use case modeling, data flow diagrams, entity-relationship modeling, and interface design. The course emphasizes effective communication with stakeholders and iterative development through case studies and team projects.

courses.name: Introduction to Image Forensics
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to introduce the students with theoretical and practical aspects of image forensics. The student is introduced to digital images, their representation and their use for steganography, steganalysis and image manipulation detection.
courses.description: This course is an introduction digital image forensics. Topics include concepts from signals and systems, information theory, lossless and lossy compression, steganography, steganalysis and manipulation detection of digital images.
learning_outcomes: Understanding of signals and systems concepts
Understanding of image representation, processing and compression
Understanding the concepts of steganography, watermarking and steganalysis
Understanding the concepts of image manipulation detection

courses.name: Distributed Systems
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce the principles and design challenges of distributed systems, where networked computers coordinate their actions to provide reliable and scalable services. It also prepares students to understand and build modern distributed applications using current technologies and architectural patterns.
courses.description: This course examines the design and implementation of distributed systems in which components on networked computers communicate and coordinate their actions. Topics include remote procedure calls (RPC), distributed file systems, consistency models, fault tolerance, and distributed algorithms. Students develop scalable and resilient systems using technologies such as gRPC, Kafka, and cloud platforms. Real-world applications include microservice architectures, blockchain systems, and distributed databases.

courses.name: Data Communications
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental principles and technologies of data communications and computer networks. It aims to provide students with a solid understanding of how data is transmitted, processed, and managed in modern communication systems.
courses.description: Topics include transmission media, coding techniques, error detection and correction methods, and network protocols. Students examine the OSI and TCP/IP models, IP addressing, routing, and switching processes. The course also includes practical laboratory work using network simulation tools and covers emerging trends such as 5G, the Internet of Things (IoT), and network security.

courses.name: Digital Signal Processing
courses.purpose: This course introduces the theory and practical applications of digital signal processing (DSP), helping students understand how discrete-time signals and systems are analyzed and processed. It aims to build both the mathematical foundation and the applied skills needed for DSP-related engineering tasks.
courses.description: The course covers discrete-time signals and systems, Fourier transforms, digital filters (FIR and IIR), and sampling theory. Students learn to analyze and process signals in the time and frequency domains using tools such as MATLAB or Python. Application areas include audio processing, image enhancement, biomedical signal analysis, and communication systems.

courses.name: Embedded Systems Programming
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the design and programming of embedded systems used in hardware-integrated computing devices. It also prepares students to develop functional embedded applications for automation, robotics, and IoT contexts.
courses.description: The course focuses on embedded systems design and programming. Students learn about microcontrollers, real-time operating systems (RTOS), memory management, and interfacing with peripheral devices. Programming is carried out in C/C++ on platforms such as Arduino, STM32, or Raspberry Pi. Topics include interrupt management, power management, and embedded communication protocols such as SPI, I2C, and UART. Projects involve developing functional embedded systems for automation, robotics, or Internet of Things (IoT) applications.

courses.name: Agile Methods in Software Development
courses.purpose: This course examines agile methodologies used for managing software development projects and helps students understand how these approaches support collaborative and iterative delivery.
courses.description: This course covers agile methodologies for software development project management. Students learn approaches such as Scrum, Kanban, Extreme Programming (XP), and Lean Software Development. Topics include user stories, sprint planning, continuous integration, and retrospective meetings. The course emphasizes collaboration, adaptability, and iterative delivery processes. Students participate in team-based projects using agile tools such as Jira or Trello and simulate real-world agile workflows and practices.

courses.name: Introduction to Data Science
courses.purpose: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to data science, covering the full data workflow from data collection to visualization.
courses.description: Students learn data manipulation, exploratory data analysis, statistical modeling, and machine learning using Python libraries such as Pandas, NumPy, Scikit-learn, and Matplotlib. The course also covers data ethics, feature engineering, model evaluation, and data storytelling. Projects involve working with real-world datasets to derive insights and build predictive models.

courses.name: System Modeling and Simulation
courses.purpose: This course introduces techniques for modeling and simulating complex systems in engineering and computing. It aims to help students build mathematical and computational models and use simulation to analyze system behavior, optimize performance, and support decision-making.
courses.description: The course covers techniques for modeling and simulation of complex systems in engineering and computing fields. Students learn to create mathematical and computational models using tools such as MATLAB/Simulink or SimPy. Topics include discrete-event simulation, continuous system modeling, queueing systems, and Monte Carlo methods. Application areas include manufacturing, logistics, healthcare, and software systems.

courses.name: Software Engineering Ethics
courses.purpose: This course examines the ethical and professional responsibilities of software engineers. It prepares students to recognize and address complex moral dilemmas they may encounter in their professional careers.
courses.description: The course covers topics such as intellectual property, privacy, cybersecurity, algorithmic bias, and the societal impact of technology. Students explore ethical frameworks, case analyses, and codes of conduct such as the ACM/IEEE Software Engineering Code of Ethics.
learning_outcomes: Understand the ethical and professional responsibilities of software engineers.
Analyze ethical issues related to intellectual property, privacy, cybersecurity, algorithmic bias, and the societal impact of technology.
Apply ethical frameworks and professional codes of conduct to real-world scenarios.
Develop critical thinking skills for making ethical decisions in professional practice.

courses.name: Introduction to Blockchain Technology
courses.purpose: The main objective is to provide students practical and theoretical foundations to use and develop applications using the blockchain technology.
courses.description: This course provides a thorough understanding of the fundamental concepts and recent advances in blockchain and cryptocurrencies. The main objective is to provide students practical and theoretical foundations to use and develop applications using the blockchain technology.
learning_outcomes: Acquisition of problem-solving and analysis skills
Acquisition of the ability to apply basic technical skills
Programming ability acquisition
Comprehend algorithm logic
Designing and writing the program that suits the needs

Hafta 2: Blockchain Fundamentals

courses.name: Computer Vision
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental concepts and techniques of computer vision so that machines can interpret and understand visual information from the world.
courses.description: The course covers image processing, feature extraction, object detection, motion tracking, and 3D reconstruction. Students explore algorithms such as edge detection, Hough transforms, and convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for image classification and segmentation. Applied areas include face recognition, autonomous vehicles, and augmented reality. In practical projects, students develop real-world visual systems using libraries such as OpenCV and TensorFlow.

courses.name: Software Testing and Maintenance
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with the principles and practices needed to ensure software quality through systematic testing and long-term maintenance strategies. It also prepares students to build and manage sustainable software systems over time.
courses.description: This course focuses on systematic testing methods and long-term maintenance strategies for ensuring software quality. Students learn unit testing, integration testing, system testing, and test automation. Topics include test-driven development (TDD), code coverage, debugging techniques, and regression testing. The course also covers software evolution, refactoring, and technical debt management. Students use tools such as JUnit, Selenium, and CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery) to build robust test frameworks and learn how to manage software systems sustainably over time.

courses.name: Introduction to Optimization
courses.purpose: This course provides an introduction to mathematical optimization techniques used to solve problems encountered in engineering and computing. It aims to help students learn how to formulate real-world problems and apply appropriate optimization algorithms.
courses.description: Topics include linear programming, nonlinear optimization, integer programming, and constraint satisfaction. Students learn to model real-world problems and apply algorithms such as the simplex method, gradient descent, and branch-and-bound. Application areas include logistics, resource allocation, machine learning, and operations research. Students also gain hands-on problem-solving experience using tools such as MATLAB, Python (SciPy), or Gurobi.
learning_outcomes: Model real-world engineering and computing problems using optimization techniques.
Apply linear programming, nonlinear optimization, integer programming, and constraint satisfaction methods.
Use algorithms such as simplex, gradient descent, and branch-and-bound to solve optimization problems.
Gain practical experience with optimization tools such as MATLAB, Python (SciPy), or Gurobi.

courses.name: Introduction to Cryptography
courses.purpose: This course examines the principles and applications of cryptography to ensure the security of data and communications.
courses.description: The course covers symmetric and asymmetric encryption, cryptographic hash functions, digital signatures, and key exchange protocols. Students learn classical encryption methods, RSA, AES, and elliptic curve cryptography, and explore real-world applications such as secure messaging, blockchain, and digital identity. The course emphasizes both theoretical foundations and practical implementation using cryptographic libraries.

courses.name: Introduction to the Internet of Things
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the architecture, technologies, and applications of the Internet of Things (IoT). It aims to provide a foundation for understanding how connected devices sense, communicate, process data, and interact with cloud services in real-world systems.
courses.description: The course covers IoT architecture, technologies, and applications. Students learn about sensors, microcontrollers such as Arduino and Raspberry Pi, communication protocols including MQTT, Zigbee, and Bluetooth, and cloud integration. Topics also include data collection, edge computing, and IoT security. Through hands-on laboratory work and projects, students design and prototype smart systems such as smart home automation, environmental monitoring, and wearable devices.

courses.name: Cloud Computing and Virtualization
courses.purpose: This course introduces the fundamental concepts and technologies of cloud computing and virtualization. It aims to help students understand how cloud services are delivered and how virtualized environments are used to deploy and manage applications.
courses.description: This course covers the basics of cloud computing and virtualization technologies. Students explore cloud service models such as IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS, as well as virtualization techniques including hypervisors and containers. The course also introduces major cloud platforms such as AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud, along with topics like resource provisioning, scalability, fault tolerance, and cloud security. Students gain practical experience in deploying applications in virtualized environments and managing cloud-based infrastructure using tools such as Docker, Kubernetes, and Terraform.

courses.name: Virtual and Augmented Reality
courses.purpose: This course examines the design and development of virtual and augmented reality experiences.
courses.description: Topics include 3D graphics, spatial interaction, tracking systems, and user interface design for VR/AR. Students use development platforms such as Unity or Unreal Engine and devices such as Oculus Rift, HTC Vive, or ARKit-enabled smartphones. Applications in areas such as games, education, health, and simulation are covered. Projects involve developing interactive VR/AR applications that blend digital content with the physical world.

courses.name: Reinforcement Learning
courses.purpose: Bu ders, öğrencilerin bir ortamla etkileşime girerek karar veren ajanların nasıl öğrenebildiğini anlamalarını sağlar. Pekiştirmeli öğrenmenin temel ilkelerini ve bu alandaki başlıca algoritmaları tanıtır.
courses.description: Bu ders, pekiştirmeli öğrenmenin temel kavramlarını ve yöntemlerini kapsar. Markov karar süreçleri, dinamik programlama, Monte Carlo yöntemleri, temporal-difference learning, Q-öğrenme ve politika gradyan yöntemleri ele alınır. Ayrıca robotik, oyun yapay zekâsı ve öneri sistemleri gibi uygulama alanları incelenir. Uygulamalı çalışmalarda Python, OpenAI Gym ve TensorFlow kullanılarak RL algoritmalarının uygulanması yapılır.

courses.name: Service-Oriented Web Programming
courses.purpose: This course aims to introduce students to the design and development of web applications using service-oriented architecture. It also helps students learn how to build, consume, and integrate web services and manage microservices in scalable applications.
courses.description: This course focuses on the design and development of web applications using service-oriented architecture (SOA). Students learn to create and consume RESTful and SOAP web services, integrate with third-party APIs, and manage microservices. Topics include JSON/XML data exchange, authentication (OAuth, JWT), and service orchestration. Projects use frameworks such as Spring Boot, Flask, or Node.js to develop scalable and modular web applications.

courses.name: Mobile Application Development
courses.purpose: This course aims to equip students with the skills needed to design and develop robust, user-friendly mobile applications for Android and iOS platforms. It also introduces modern mobile architecture patterns, performance considerations, offline capabilities, and cloud integration.
courses.description: This is an advanced mobile programming course focused on developing applications for Android and iOS. Students learn mobile architecture patterns such as MVC and MVVM, performance optimization, offline functionality, and integration with cloud services. Application development is carried out using native SDKs with Kotlin and Swift, and cross-platform tools such as Flutter or React Native are also covered. The course concludes with a capstone project that includes a fully functional mobile application.

courses.name: Advanced Linux
courses.purpose: This course aims to provide students with in-depth knowledge of the Linux operating system and prepare them for advanced roles in DevOps, systems engineering, and cybersecurity.
courses.description: This course covers advanced topics in the Linux operating system, including system administration, shell scripting, kernel configuration, and performance tuning. It also addresses file systems, process management, network configuration, security, and virtualization. Students gain hands-on experience with tools such as systemd, cron, iptables, and Docker.

courses.name: Information Retrieval and Web Search
courses.purpose: This course examines the principles and technologies behind search engines and information retrieval systems. It aims to help students understand how information is indexed, ranked, and retrieved, and how modern search systems are built and evaluated.
courses.description: The course covers indexing, ranking algorithms, relevance feedback, natural language queries, and web crawling. Students learn retrieval models such as Boolean, vector space, and probabilistic approaches. In the practical parts, search engines are developed and experimental studies are conducted using tools such as Elasticsearch and Apache Lucene.

courses.name: Software Requirements and Analysis
courses.purpose: This course introduces students to the early phases of the software development life cycle with a focus on requirements engineering and system analysis. It aims to develop the ability to elicit, document, validate, and manage software requirements in practical settings.
courses.description: The course covers requirements engineering and system analysis topics, including use case modeling, stakeholder analysis, traceability, and requirements prioritization. It also includes real-world case studies and team-based projects that simulate customer interactions and requirements definition processes.

courses.name: Machine Learning
courses.purpose: The aim of this course is to ensure that students understand the fundamental concepts, algorithms, and application processes of machine learning. The course covers basic machine learning algorithms both theoretically and practically, and helps students understand the role of machine learning in engineering, informatics, and data science.
courses.description: This course covers basic concepts and historical development of machine learning, supervised and unsupervised learning algorithms, linear and logistic regression, K-nearest neighbor algorithm, support vector machines, decision trees and random forests, clustering methods, dimensionality reduction methods, model evaluation and performance metrics, and overfitting and validation strategies.
learning_outcomes: An ability to understand the fundamental concepts and techniques of machine learning
Understand supervised learning and apply classification/regression.
Explain linear and logistic regression techniques.
Recognize non-linear algorithms such as decision trees, KNN, and SVM.
Evaluate data partitioning, flexibility, and accuracy in tree-based methods.

Hafta 2: Supervised Learning

Hafta 7: Support Vector Machines & Naive Bayes
Hafta 8: Midterm Exam
Hafta 9: Tree-Based Methods
Hafta 10: Tree-Based Methods (cont’d)
Hafta 11: Ensemble Learning
